comments.content:
[IMAGE:0]It's getting on for midnight, so here we go with a new Magic Monday. The picture is of George Van Tassel, another of the California occultists who played a central role in the creation of the UFO phenomenon. The head of the College of Eternal Wisdom, one of the many occult schools in mid-20th century California, Van Tassel was a friend or associate of just about everyone involved in the early contactee scene, and the annual meetings at his property at Giant Rock, CA were seedbeds for the entire New Age scene -- though you'll find few people in that scene who will talk about that any more. 

[IMAGE:1]If you're in the vicinity, Giant Rock is worth a visit, partly for the scenery, partly for the history, and partly for the Integratron, a domed building Van Tassel said he was directed to build by the aliens. Made of wood and other non-ferromagnetic substances and incorporating a gigantic Tesla coil and split-ring resonator, it was supposed to rejuvenate those who spent time in it. I haven't been there, though I've been told by some who have that it's got really interesting energies -- yes, it's still in existence, still in use for mystical purposes, and was added to the National Register of Historic Places in 2018. That's George standing near the Integratron in the picture to the right. 

Ask me anything about occultism and I'll do my best to answer it. Any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer, though it may be Tuesday sometime before I get to them all. If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 143,916th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.0 of The Magic Monday FAQ here.

I've had several people ask about tipping me for answers here, and though I certainly don't require that I won't turn it down. You can use the button below to access my online tip jar.

With that said, have at it!

***This Magic Monday is now closed to new questions. See you next week!***

I would say that our memories and consciousness have pretty much reintegrated at this point, although somethings my alter was good at I still am not good at. Candace

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
My take on multiple personalities in general is that (almost?) every adolescent and adult has a considerable number of distinct personalities, but is more or less aware of them and can transition from one to another smoothly as circumstances appear to require. It's when the transitions between personalities are neither smooth nor appropriate to circumstances that it becomes a disorder. It becomes even more a disorder when some of the personalities are hidden, or hide themselves, from the rest, or they war among themselves for control of the body which they all have in common.

Some people have termed them "alternate selves" (instead of multiple personalities). That terminology goes back well into the 19th century. Charles Godfrey Leland wrote in his "The Mystic Will" (1899, originally titled "Have You a Strong Will") about a dream self living in him whom he did not care for at all, but who persisted in crating vile dreams while he slept. Of these two selves in him, he wrote, "If we belonged to the same club, we would not speak."

In those same years, Frederic W. H. Myers was working up his theory of human "subliminal selves"--that is, selves below the threshold of one's consciousness--in a series of articles in the "Proceedings of the Society for Psychical Research" (1892-1895), and then in his masterwork, "Human Personality and Its Survival of Bodily Death" (1903). In his day, Myers was a potent force to be reckoned with not just in psychic research, but also in psychology and in occultism. He clearly influenced both Freud and Jung. (Jung kept a copy of the 1906 French translation of Myers' "Human Personality" in his own private library.)

Half a century later, Fritz Leiber, the noted SF author, theorized in a number of interviews and essays about the multiple selves--far more than just two--that co-existed in (almost?) all normal people. He also thought this was an excellent thing for a writer to have.

I absorbed much the same point of view in my own family while I was growing up in the SF Bay area: each normal person had many selves, though usually only one of them is awake at any given time; the others sleep in a sort of cellar. Each self has its own personality, its strengths and weaknesses in dealing with the real world in which the body has to live. Skilled people have paid attention to a number of their selves while each of them is awake, and they know the strengths and weaknesses of each of their selves. Such people can wake up an appropriate self to deal with each new situation, while sending the previous "waking self" back to sleep for a while. Also, pretty much everyone has at least one, and maybe several depraved or vicious "sleeping selves" that should never be awakened at all, much less be freed from the cellar. And finally, a person who really does have just one single self is a sort of cripple, more to be pitied than emulated.

Some of the earlier history of the study of multiple selves has been elucidated quite well by Adam Crabtree in his book "Multiple Man: Explorations in Possession and Multiple Personality" (1985).

For whatever it may be worth, my own family's occultism was not influenced by H. P. Blavatsky, much less by Eliphas Levy. Thus we did not have anything to say about various planes of existence (material, astral, mental, etc.). So I'm not really competent to address that question of yours.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
The best book I've found so far on rectification is Laurie Efrein's How to Rectify a Birth Chart (Aquarian, 1987), which is readily available via online used book stores.

If you've got the date, you've at least got all the planetary placements other than the Moon, and the Moon's within 15 degrees or so. You may be able to narrow things down quite a bit if the Moon passes from one sign to another during the interval -- the difference between a Cancer Moon and a Leo Moon, say, is hard to miss! Beyond that, look for telltale house placements. Someone who has really good financial luck will probably have a benefic in the 2nd house or ruling the 2nd house cusp, for example, and if you piece all these together you may well be able to get it within a fairly narrow window. Then cast the chart for the middle of the window and see how well it fits!

comments.title: Freyja, Odin
comments.content: Why does Norse mythology have Freyja and Odin each taking half of the slain? How did each choose?

replies.title: Re: Freyja, Odin
replies.content: You'll have to ask someone who worships them. I don't claim to be any sort of scholar of the Eddas.

replies.title: Re: Freyja, Odin
replies.content:
There are at least two ways one can make some sense of this bit of old lore.

(1) Just as Odin is the foremost among the Æsir Gods, so Freyja is foremost among the Vanir Gods. In the beginning, the Æsir and the Vanir were at war with one another, but eventually they came to terms of peace, began to work together, and--perhaps--intermarried. (Odin's wife is Frigga, while Freyja's husband is Od. This has led to questions about the possible identity of Odin and Od, and of Freyja and Frigga.)

It's quite reasonable to suppose that an equal division of the slain between the two families of Gods would be part of their peace terms at the end of that first war.

We don't know all that much about the Vanir. Snorri Sturluson, to whom we owe the preservation of most of the old lore that has come down to us, was pretty clearly interested in the Æsir far more than in the Vanir. But even the little that we do know about the Vanir suggests that their "social organization" was more "matriarchial" than that of the Æsir. So it makes sense that the Vanic half of the slain would go to Freyja rather than to her brother Freyr.

By the way, Freyja and Freyr are simply the words meaning "Lady" and "Lord." Freyja's actual names, assuming she had them, have not come down to us at all. In contrast, one of Freyr's names, Yngvi, seems to have come down to us.


(2) It's an ancient observation--though not really a very accurate one--that some men are primarily fighters, while others are primarily lovers (though they can also fight). Perhaps it seemed right to the ancient Norse that Odin would get the fighters among the slain and Freyja the lovers.

replies.title: Re: Freyja, Odin
replies.content: Makes sense, though Freya probably got more fun out of the deal!

Oriens, Ayamon, Paymon and Egim are the names of four demonic kings of the Elements, associated also with the four points of the compass. Rituals to invoke them go back at least as far as the early centuries of the High Middle Ages, and probably a lot farther. They probably had something to do with the very learned astronomer/astrologer Cecco d'Ascoli's getting himself burned at the stake in 1327.

Later they figure in the rituals laid down in the notorious handbook "The Astrologer of the Nineteenth Century" (1825), complied by a (formerly) secret society of occultists known as The Mercurii. Robert Cross Smith, aka Raphael the Astrologer, was probably the chief mover of this publication.

Like our host, I wouldn't have anything to do with those four for all the proverbial tea in China and everywhere else in the world.

replies.title: Re: crosses and visualizations
replies.content:
In response to 4:

Frater Ashen Chassan, a modern (Christian) grimoric magician (and clearly a very competent one), has published a tome called "Gateways Through Light and Shadow" which is a comprehensive record of summonings he and his scryer did, mostly of various angels. The later section of the book includes summonings of the four cardinal rulers. He was given instruction and guidance on the summonings by the archangel Sandalphon. Of the Cardinal Rulers, Sandalphon says:
"As we are the vertical, they are the horizontal. They open gates that allow the plane of experiences to converge on the circle of the magician. Associating with their direction, they pull the qualities of that space and send it to those who call in a holy matter for holy intentions, and for those who call for bad or evil intentions. They are without judgement, without morality. They give their energies forth simply to give, not to judge."

I definitely got the sense from the book that the Cardinal Rulers are fundamental and neutral beings, and the function principally as gatekeepers of the cardinals. That any time anyone calls on the power of one of the four elements or four directions, that power ultimately comes through one of these beings.

Based on the first sentence of the quote from Sandalphon above, I spent several months using Sanalphon, Metatron, and the Cardinal Rulers as the forces I called in my Sphere of Protection, using the names, attributions, and appearances as given in the book. I definitely felt their presence clearly, and while I was glad to have the moralizing influence of Sandalphon and Metatron, I did not feel malefic influence from the Cardinal Rulers, and will almost certainly work with them again.

Based on Frater Ashen Chassan's accounts - not to mention my own experience - I'm by no means convinced that the Cardinal Rulers are demonic. They may be vast, primordial, and amoral, but so is the ocean. (Of course, sea magic is dangerous, so your mileage may vary)

replies.title: Re: Sigils

1) Sigils work; they're a very simple magical technique that most people can learn to use with a little practice, and I find they work about as well as affirmations or visualized thought-forms. comments.title: LBRP/feng shui

It's just another Magic Monday... (Wish it was Sunday...'cause that's my fun day!)

But Sunday wasn't my fun day...my father-in-law showed up about 10 am yesterday all but unannounced. With a friend we didn't know and weren't expecting. My kids don't care for that particular grandparent and his friend definitely moved the needle on my skeezometer, but we did our best to be kind and accommodating.

Until the conversation, after about 15 beers each for them, turned to how I wasn't keeping a close enough eye on my daughter, and how there are abductors and rapists lurking behind every azalea, and hadn't I heard about so-and-so who went missing for 6 months, and...

I tried to quell his fears: you know, we live in a tight-knit neighborhood in a small town where everyone looks after each other. Our daughter is a very strong and independent girl with 2 years of jiu jitsu under her belt, and knows every shopkeeper within walking distance of our house. How I refuse to be a helicopter parent. Etc.

The more I deflected his concerns the stronger his objections got. Until I just walked away and did something else. It frankly creeped me out. What you contemplate you imitate and all that.

I've been a devoted student of yours for over 7 years now, practiced feng shui at least as long, practiced natural magic and divination for over a year, and have recently taken up a daily LBRP. As in, for about a week now.

One thing my wife and I noticed about her dad and his friend was that they seemed to have a really hard time coming inside our house. Or staying in for long if they managed to make it inside. They were late to both meals we set out yesterday and also first to be excused.

It's hard to imagine that a week of banishing rituals is responsible. Maybe more the feng shui...but neither of them could bear to relax inside our house. They had planned to spend 2 nights with us but stormed out drunk around 10 last night, headed for Florida, acting like gibbering toddlers, neither having even laid down on the beds we made up for them. And I have to wonder if that was the last time we will see my wife's absentee father (he went to prison when she was 8 and didn't reconnect for 20 years. Been an annual visitor since we had children.)

Mostly I just needed to vent, so thanks for being here. But how likely do you figure it is that our protective efforts chased bad guys out of our house last night? It was a bizarre experience to say the least!

Tripp

replies.title: Re: LBRP/feng shui
replies.content:
Hoo boy!!! Tripp, that really sets all my alarm bells ringing loudly.

Your FIL's friend could easily have been several men in older generations in my family. ("Girls, run and hide! Here comes you father!" great-grandmother would call out to my mother and her sister every time she saw their father approaching their house.) I'm really sorry you and your family have to face this.

As I read the behavior you've described, your FIL's friend was fantasizing about raping your daughter the whole time they were at your house, and he was hoping all the alcohol would give him the courage to act on his fantasy. If I had to guess further, your FIL was trying to dissuade him, but might also have been somewhat conflicted, as his friend might have managed to infect him, too, with the fantasy of violent sex with your daughter. If that was so, then, the friend was probably the dominant man in their friendship, not your FIL.

Even so, I suspect considerable props are due your FIL for resisting his friend's pressure and refusing to go along with the idea. It seems likely that they were quarreling bitterly beneath the surface, and that their early departure was due to your FIL's refusal to give in to his friend.

If it were my daughter and she were old enough to begin (even to begin!) to understand such things, I'd have a very very frank talk with her about this danger, and particularly about how for a considerable number of men violence itself is often orgasmic quite apart from sexual attraction, and in consequence violent sex is far more satisfying for such men than any sort of consensual sex. And that she should beware of your FIL's friend if ever she so much as sees him somewhere in the distance.

It would almost certainly be helpful for your wife to hear this, and especially the part about orgasmic violence and violent sex, too; a certain number of grown women seem not to be as aware as one might wish of that detail of male psychology of orgasm.

I'd also be on the lookout, too, for your FIL's friend to show up (quite likely without your FIL!) near your house more than once in the future.

If you can, do find out and make note of the friend's name and where he lives. Your FIL might even tell you, if you handle it right, when his friend is not around. He, too, may want to protect his granddaughter from his friend.

comments.title: Why do gods care?
comments.content:
This is a question prompted by last week's discussion of the Hekate ritual for abortion rights.
What would impel a deity to act in matters that affect millions of people? Are they inclined to act because they have a stake in the matter? If so, what happens when there are other deities who might have different opinions (even those that people believe are "false")?

I can imagine that a deity might intercede in personal affairs for someone who worships them on a regular basis and has developed a relationship with them, but I find it hard to believe the gods are so lacking in attention that they would change the fate of nations in exchange for a candle or a bit of in sense smoke once in a while by a handful of people.

I'm trying to decide if it's silly and fruitless or incredibly dangerous to ask gods to get involved with political matters.

Thank you by the way for your generosity in answering these questions each week.

N

replies.title: Re: Why do gods care?
replies.content:
We don't know why the gods do what they do. Remember that gods are far more intelligent than we are, and our chance of understanding why they do things are about on a par with someone's pet goldfish understanding their owner's financial affairs or relationship issues!

Prayer is not about making the gods do things for you. It's about entering into the presence of the gods, and listening is more important in prayer than talking. A deity that has some kind of stake in a given matter might choose to intervene, but no, they're not going to do it because some human being asks them to -- they're going to do it because they choose to.

That said, you can place your worries and fears and hopes for the future before them and ask for their guidance and help. Don't tell them what to do about it, just turn to them as you would to a far older and wiser friend. In my experience, that gets much better results.

replies.title: Re: Magic of the gaps?
replies.content:
I tend to describe the difference between magic and psychology as follows:

-- psychology consists of those methods for understanding and working with the inner life of the individual that have been created since 1850 or so under the influence of modern scientific materialism.

-- occultism consists of those methods for understanding and working wit hthe inner life of the individual that have been passed down since ancient times by way of Renaissance Hermeticism, and which have resisted the influence of modern scientific materialism.

Where exactly the boundaries lie, which one is best suited to what aspect of human understanding, and so on -- all that still has to be worked out by experiment.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
No, abstraction is the peculiar property of the mental plane, and our concepts are attempts to echo the pure abstract understandings of the mental plane in astral terms our minds can handle more clearly -- that's why every concept has a verbal label (that is, a vocal sound) associated with it and at least some degree of mental imagery as well.

As for divination, here again, you're getting the planes scrambled. To perform a divination, you use a randomizing process to let the creative patterns that move down the planes from spiritual to material produce a set of material forms -- a layout of tarot cards, say. You then use your knowledge of the cards' meanings (which is astral) and your intuition (which is the first glimmering of awareness of the mental plane) to figure out the broader pattern of events that the reading reflects.
